coredata.dc:title: Recent trends in use of plant-derived carbon dot-based fluorescent probes for heavy metal ion detection and their biological applications

coredata.dc:description: Plant-derived carbon dots (CDs) have drawn a lot of interest as superior nanomaterials because of their sustainable nature, biocompatibility, and environmentally friendly synthesis, which sets them apart from other CDs made from non-renewable resources. The synthesis of CDs from natural sources such as fruits, leaves, bark, stem, flower, seed and biowaste by hydrothermal, pyrolysis, and microwave-assisted method have been investigated to modify the size and features of CDs. The plant-derived CDs exhibit greater sensitivity and selectivity, and their high photoluminescent properties makes it possible to detect physiological and ecological significant metal ions like iron (Fe³⁺), copper (Cu²⁺), lead (Pb²⁺), and mercury (Hg²⁺). This study focuses mainly on crucial elements such as absorption, emission, sensitivity, selectivity, and limits of detection of metal ions, a thorough examination of their sensing characteristics is provided. This review article comprehensively summarizes plant-based CDs with different synthesis methods, characterization techniques, metal ion sensing and its mechanism, stability and biological imaging of CDs. Additionally, we discussed the plant derived CDs for the detection of pesticides and drugs. These CDs also find extensive applications in environmental remediation by offering a sustainable alternative for detecting harmful pollutants. Beyond these sensing, their biological uses of plant-derived CDs, such as bioimaging, antioxidant activity, and therapeutic potential, are finally addressed. This review emphasizes the bright future of plant-derived CDs in biomedicine and sustainable nanotechnology, with a focus on their biological applications and improved sensing capabilities in research development.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Plant-derived carbon dots (CDs) have drawn a lot of interest as superior nanomaterials because of their sustainable nature, biocompatibility, and environmentally friendly synthesis, which sets them apart from other CDs made from non-renewable resources. The synthesis of CDs from natural sources such as fruits, leaves, bark, stem, flower, seed and biowaste by hydrothermal, pyrolysis, and microwave-assisted method have been investigated to modify the size and features of CDs. The plant-derived CDs exhibit greater sensitivity and selectivity, and their high photoluminescent properties makes it possible to detect physiological and ecological significant metal ions like iron (Fe³⁺), copper (Cu²⁺), lead (Pb²⁺), and mercury (Hg²⁺). This study focuses mainly on crucial elements such as absorption, emission, sensitivity, selectivity, and limits of detection of metal ions, a thorough examination of their sensing characteristics is provided. This review article comprehensively summarizes plant-based CDs with different synthesis methods, characterization techniques, metal ion sensing and its mechanism, stability and biological imaging of CDs. Additionally, we discussed the plant derived CDs for the detection of pesticides and drugs. These CDs also find extensive applications in environmental remediation by offering a sustainable alternative for detecting harmful pollutants. Beyond these sensing, their biological uses of plant-derived CDs, such as bioimaging, antioxidant activity, and therapeutic potential, are finally addressed. This review emphasizes the bright future of plant-derived CDs in biomedicine and sustainable nanotechnology, with a focus on their biological applications and improved sensing capabilities in research development.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a novel form of zero-dimensional nanomaterial that has sparked significant attention in the field of nanotechnology since its discovery by Xu et al. in 2004 [1]. These nanoparticles, typically less than 10 nm in size, exhibit unique properties such as high photoluminescence, excellent water solubility, low toxicity, outstanding biocompatibility and versatile synthesis making them highly suitable for a wide range of applications [2,3]. Composed primarily of carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen, CDs can be synthesized from a wide array of carbon sources, such as organic molecules, polymers, and biomass [4]. CDs derived from natural sources, such as food waste, animal products, plants, and bio-waste, have antibacterial, antioxidant, and other advantageous biological properties [5–8]. One of the most intriguing aspects of CDs is their tunable photoluminescent properties, which can be adjusted by modifying their size, surface functional groups, and heteroatom doping [9,10].

Furthermore, CDs have shown importance in environmental applications such as the detection and removal of heavy metal ions, organic contaminants, and other hazardous substances [11,12]. Their small size makes them ability to form complexes with metal ions leading to changes in photoluminescence properties. They can detect metal ions like iron (Fe3 +), copper (Cu2+), lead (Pb²⁺), mercury (Hg²⁺), and cadmium (Cd²⁺) with high sensitivity and selectivity and their high surface area enable efficient interaction with contaminants, making them effective for environmental monitoring and remediation. CDs also play a major role in numerous biological applications like drug delivery, gene delivery and bio sensing which supports in better health outcomes and explore the boundaries of biomedical sciences [13]. For biological applications, mostly biocompatible and hydrophilic materials are in demand extensively. CDs are highly soluble in water which make them suitable for bio imaging applications. Because of these characteristics, they are perfect for use as stable, probes in fluorescence-based bio imaging, which allows the visualization of biological processes and structures.

The synthesis of CDs can be approached through two primary methods: top-down and bottom-up. The top-down method involves breaking down larger materials into nanoscale structures via chemical or physical techniques [14]. In bottom-up approaches, the CDs are obtained from assembling ultra-small molecular precursors under a range of different reaction conditions [3]. Recently, artificial intelligence techniques like machine learning have been explored to enhance the synthesis and properties of CDs. However, challenges remain in optimizing reaction parameters such as temperature and precursor selection to achieve desired characteristics efficiently [15]. Existing literature has extensively explored the synthesis of CDs from various sources, covering aspects such as their characterization, physicochemical properties, large-scale production, and diverse applications. However, there is limited analysis that specifically integrates and examines the unique properties of plant-derived CDs, particularly their combined potential for biological applications and metal ion sensing. Addressing this gap could provide valuable insights into the multifunctionality and broader applicability of plant-based CDs in these fields [16–18]. This article provides a comprehensive review of the progress made in the synthesis, characterization, and application of plant-derived CDs for metal ion sensing and various biological applications. We will first examine the synthesis methods of CDs from natural plant sources, followed by their characterization techniques. Additionally, we will discuss their optical, electrical, and chemical properties, as well as their effectiveness in metal ion sensing. The stability of CDs under various conditions will also be examined. Finally, we will highlight the current prominent uses of CDs in metal ion detection and biological applications.
The synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) can be broadly categorized into two main approaches: top-down and bottom-up methods ( Fig. 1).

Top-down methods involve breaking larger carbon precursors into smaller CD particles. These techniques often require energy-intensive procedures to achieve the desired size and properties of CDs. Notable top-down techniques are discussed below [19,20].

Laser ablation: Laser ablation is a versatile and highly efficient top-down method for the green synthesis of CDs. In this technique, a focused laser beam is directed at a carbon-rich material, such as graphite, suspended in a green solvent. The intense laser energy causes ablation, fragmentation, and vaporization of the carbon source, resulting in the formation of CDs. For example, Donate et al [21]. demonstrated the synthesis of CDs by irradiating carbon powder dispersed in a solvent as in Fig. 2a. Laser ablation is highly praised for its rapid production, scalability, and eco-friendliness. CDs synthesized through this method have applications in bioimaging, drug delivery, and optoelectronics [22–29].

Arc discharge: Arc discharge is another green and effective method for synthesizing CDs. This technique involves passing high electrical currents through carbon-rich materials, such as graphite rods, in an inert gas environment to generate a plasma arc. The extreme temperatures and pressures in the arc lead to the fragmentation and vaporization of the carbon source, producing carbon nanoparticles, including CDs. Notably, this method avoids the use of hazardous chemicals and solvents, resulting in minimal toxic by-products. Additionally, the use of simple and widely available carbon sources makes it an eco-friendly approach to CD synthesis. An illustration of this method is shown in Fig. 2b [30–32].

Electrochemical oxidation: The electrochemical oxidation method synthesizes CDs by oxidizing carbon precursors under an applied electric potential. This technique is eco-friendly and efficient, producing CDs with customizable optical and electronic properties. For instance, light-yellow CDs were synthesized from a 0.5 M glycine solution in 10 mL of 3 M ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH) at 25 °C using an electrochemical method for 2 h. By applying a 10 V voltage between two platinum electrodes, glycine underwent oxidation, forming immonium ions. These ions reacted with unoxidized glycine molecules, followed by electro-polymerization and carbonization, leading to highly FL CDs (Fig. 2d). The process involves a carbon-based working electrode, such as graphite or carbon fibers, immersed in an electrolyte solution containing a supporting salt like sodium sulfate or potassium chloride. When an electric potential is applied between the working and counter electrodes, the carbon precursor at the working electrode undergoes oxidation, resulting in its fragmentation and the formation of CDs in the electrolyte. CDs synthesized through this method are highly valued for applications in bioimaging, sensing, energy storage, and catalysis due to their excellent biocompatibility, tunable optical properties, and unique electronic characteristics [33–35].

Acidic oxidation: Acidic oxidation involves breaking down larger carbon precursors into smaller particles using strong acids. For instance, nanosized carbon particles derived from Cucumis melo fruit were treated with sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄) and phosphoric acid (H₃PO₄) to produce multicolor-emissive CDs (Fig. 2e). This method leverages the oxidative power of acids to cleave carbon-carbon bonds, yielding CDs with controlled sizes and surface properties. A subsequent passivation step, often with agents like polyethylene glycol (PEG), improves the photoluminescence and stability of the CDs. The simplicity, cost-effectiveness, and accessibility of this method make it attractive for large-scale and eco-friendly CD production [37,38].

Chemical oxidation: The chemical oxidation method is a technique used to produce CDs by oxidizing and decomposing larger carbon precursors using strong oxidizing agents. In this process, carbon-rich materials such as graphite, carbon black, or activated carbon are treated with oxidizing agents like concentrated nitric acid (HNO₃) or sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄). The precursor is typically heated in the presence of these acids, which cleave carbon-carbon bonds and break the material into smaller carbon fragments. For example, carbon black precursors are reflux-heated at 110 °C for 24 h using 6 M HNO₃ with continuous stirring. After this oxidation step, the mixture is neutralized with sodium hydroxide (NaOH). The resulting product is then purified through ultrafiltration and dialysis over 360 h (Fig. 2c). During this process, functional groups such as carboxyl (-COOH), hydroxyl (-OH), and nitro (-NO₂) are introduced to the surface of the carbon material, enhancing its properties [39]. One of the major advantages of the chemical oxidation method is its ability to produce CDs with high quantum yield (QY) and excellent photoluminescence properties, making it suitable for a wide range of applications [40].

Ultrasonic synthesis: Ultrasonic synthesis is a top-down method for producing CDs using high-frequency sound waves to break down larger carbon precursors into smaller particles. This technique is known for its simplicity and environmentally friendly nature, as it typically avoids harsh chemicals. The process begins by dissolving a carbon-rich precursor, such as glucose, citric acid, or sucrose, in water or another suitable solvent. The solution is then exposed to ultrasonic waves, which generate cavitation bubbles. The collapse of these bubbles releases intense energy, facilitating the carbonization of the precursor and the formation of CDs. For example, M-CDs were synthesized by treating kiwifruit juice with various additives like ethanol (EA), acetone (ACTA), and ethylenediamine (EDA) using ultrasonic methods ( Fig. 3f) [41]. Ultrasonic synthesis offers several advantages, including its eco-friendly nature, rapid synthesis, and mild reaction conditions, which help maintain the structural integrity of the carbon quantum dots [41–44].

Hydrothermal synthesis: Hydrothermal synthesis is a widely used bottom-up method for producing CDs, known for its ability to generate CDs under high temperature and pressure in a sealed environment. In this process, a carbon-rich precursor, such as citric acid, glucose, or other organic compounds, is dissolved in water or suitable solvent and placed in a sealed autoclave. The autoclave is then heated to temperatures typically between 150 °C and 250 °C, for several hours to days. The elevated temperature and pressure facilitate the carbonization and dehydration of the precursor, leading to CD formation through chemical reactions like polymerization and condensation. As shown in Fig. 3a, the resulting CDs have a carbon core surrounded by oxygen-containing functional groups on their surface [45]. One of the main advantages of hydrothermal synthesis is its ability to precisely control the size and surface properties of the CDs, making it a versatile and effective method for producing high-quality CDs [46–49].

Microwave assisted synthesis: Microwave-assisted synthesis is a rapid and efficient method for producing CDs by using microwave irradiation to accelerate the carbonization of precursors. In this technique, a carbon-rich precursor such as glucose, starch, or citric acid is dissolved in a suitable solvent, typically water [50]. The solution is then exposed to microwave energy, as shown in Fig. 3b, which heats the precursor quickly and uniformly. This rapid heating promotes the decomposition of the precursor, leading to the formation of CDs. One of the key advantages of microwave-assisted synthesis is its speed. Unlike traditional thermal methods, the entire process can be completed in just a few minutes, making it a significantly faster alternative. This method is not only efficient but also versatile, offering potential for environmentally sustainable practices in CD production [50–54].

Pyrolysis: Pyrolysis is a synthesis method for producing CDs through the thermal decomposition of carbon-rich precursors in an inert atmosphere. In this technique, materials such as biomass, sugar, or other organic compounds are heated to high temperatures, typically between 300 °C and 1000 °C, in the absence of oxygen. This prevents combustion and allows for the controlled breakdown of the precursor into smaller carbon structures, leading to the formation of CDs. For example, blue fluorescent CDs-1 and yellow fluorescent CDs-2 were synthesized by pyrolyzing a mixture of citric acid (CA) and urea in closed and open environments, respectively, as illustrated in Fig. 3d. During pyrolysis, the precursor undergoes chemical transformations, including dehydration, depolymerization, and carbonization, which result in carbon nanoparticles with distinct optical and electronic properties. A notable advantage of pyrolysis synthesis is its ability to use a variety of carbon-rich materials, including waste biomass. This enhances the sustainability of the process and reduces its environmental impact by utilizing readily available, renewable resources [55–58].

Photochemical synthesis: Photochemical synthesis is a technique for producing CDs that uses light energy to drive chemical reactions involving carbon precursors. In this method, a carbon-rich precursor, such as citric acid, glucose, or other organic compounds, is dissolved in a suitable solvent, typically water. The solution is then exposed to ultraviolet (UV) or visible light, triggering photochemical reactions that lead to the formation of CDs. The light energy excites the precursor molecules, causing them to fragment and polymerize into nanoscale carbon structures. For instance, CDs produced through the photochemical treatment of a

sucrose/NaOH/poly(ethylene glycol) system are monodisperse, with an average size of 8 nm, as shown in Fig. 3e [60]. One of the key advantages of photochemical synthesis is its ability to produce CDs with desirable optical properties, such as tunable fluorescence, by varying the wavelength and intensity of the light source. Additionally, this method allows for the incorporation of functional groups onto the CD surfaces during the reaction, which enhances their stability and biocompatibility [63].

Combustion synthesis: Combustion synthesis is a technique for producing CDs that relies on the rapid, exothermic reaction of carbon-rich precursors. In this process, a carbon source such as citric acid, urea, or glycerol is heated to high temperatures, typically between 300 °C and 500 °C, in the presence of an oxidizing agent. The intense heat triggers the combustion of the precursor, releasing a substantial amount of energy and generating CDs as a byproduct. This rapid combustion process promotes the formation of CDs with small sizes and high crystallinity. For instance, green fluorescent boron-nitrogen doped CDs (B, N-CDs) were synthesized using combustion synthesis for the detection of Cu²⁺ ions, as illustrated in Fig. 3c. Combustion synthesis is valued for its simplicity and efficiency, requiring minimal equipment. It enables swift production, making it ideal for applications in bioimaging, sensing, and optoelectronics [59].

The choice of synthesis method for CDs depends on various factors, including the desired properties of the CDs, the availability of resources, and the specific application requirements. Top-down methods, such as laser ablation and arc discharge, provide precise control over CD characteristics but may involve more complex setups. In contrast, bottom-up methods, including hydrothermal and microwave-assisted synthesis, offer simpler and more scalable approaches, often utilizing renewable resources. Each method has its own advantages and limitations, contributing to the diverse landscape of CD synthesis techniques ( Table 1).

A variety of spectroscopic methods provide information on the electrical structure, chemical composition, and excited state dynamics of CDs are necessary for understanding these characteristics. An essential method for evaluating the absorption spectra of CDs is UV-Vis spectroscopy, which provides important details regarding the electronic transitions and functional group presence of these particles. Researchers can determine the energy levels within CDs and their interactions with light by examining the absorption peaks; this information is critical for applications like optical sensing and photothermal treatment. The C = C bonds in the aromatic structure are attributed to the π→π∗ transition, which has a peak at 230–235 nm. This π→π∗ transition red shifts from 231–235 nm to 265–270 nm. Conversely, CDs exhibit two separate peaks at approximately 200–280 nm and 280–400 nm, which correspond to the n→π∗ transitions of C = O and the core π→π∗ transition of aromatic C = C bonds, respectively. This technique facilitates the determination of whether a ground-state complex forms between CDs and an external substrate, a characteristic important for understanding the binding mechanisms involved in diverse applications [64,65].

An important instrument used for assessing the emission spectra and QY of CDs is fluorescence spectroscopy. QY a measure of fluorescence efficiency, is especially important for high-sensitivity applications like environmental monitoring and cellular imaging. When stimulated, CDs with a high QY guarantee a significant quantity of light emission, which makes them useful probes for identifying chemical and biological species. Furthermore, the duration of the FL emission is measured using Time-Resolved Fluorescence Spectroscopy, which provides information about the dynamics of excited states. Understanding energy transfer mechanisms and CD interactions with other molecules is crucial for using them in sensing and imaging applications. To gain insight into the chemical composition and bonding of CDs, functional groups on their surface are analyzed using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). This method provides light on how surface alterations might improve the characteristics of CDs, including their biocompatibility and capacity for targeted drug delivery. The thickness or topographic profile of the particles can be determined using atomic force microscopy (AFM), which is useful for understanding the morphology and topology of the particles. AFM can offer information regarding the diameter and morphology of the particles in the case of GQDs, it also gives information regarding ratio and number of graphene layers. Raman Spectroscopy provides comprehensive insights into the vibrational modes of molecules within CDs, revealing structural characteristics and the extent of graphitization. Researchers can evaluate the quality of the carbon structure, which has an immediate influence on the optical and electrical characteristics of CDs, due to the existence of the D-band and G-band in the Raman spectra. For example, the GQDs Raman spectra display two peaks at 1337 and 1583 cm−1 that correspond to the G band, which is attributed to the vibrations of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms, and the D band, which is attributed to the vibrations of carbon with defects in the lattice, respectively. The ID/IG ratio was approximately 0.83, which is near to the graphite like structure. When maximizing CDs for different applications, the degree of disorder in the carbon structure is assessed using the intensity ratio of these bands (ID/IG).

Another effective method for figuring out the crystallinity of carbon nanomaterials is XRD. For instance, the 002 planes of CDs cause the XRD diffractograms to display large peaks centered at 2 θ= ∼21° due to graphite planes. Because of the oxygen-containing functionalities, the material's disorder or amorphous nature is indicated by the broad peak and comparatively high value of interlayer distance was observed at 0.34 nm [6]. TEM is an effective technique used to characterize CDs because it provides high-resolution imaging capabilities that allow for a thorough analysis of their morphological and structural characteristics. A crucial instrument for learning more about the size, structure, and crystallinity of CDs. A thin sample of CDs is subjected to TEM analysis using an electron beam, and the ensuing high-resolution images reveal details about the sizes, distributions, and aggregation tendencies of each individual particle. The diffraction patterns produced during TEM investigation can be used to identify the crystalline structure and lattice spacing. Additionally, TEM enables researchers to investigate the CDs surface changes and defects which affect their chemical and physical characteristics [6].

SEM is one of the useful techniques for investigating the morphological and structural features of CDs at a high resolution. SEM characterisation focussed mainly on scanning an electron beam across the sample's surface and identifying the corresponding signals, such as secondary electrons, backscattered electrons, and characteristic X-rays, in order to give a comprehensive representation of the sample topography [6]. By revealing details on the dimensions, form, and surface morphology of CDs, this method sheds light on their structural characteristics. SEM imaging can show if individual CDs are present as well as their aggregation patterns, which are important factors for nanotechnology applications.

One effective method for examining the chemical states and elemental composition of CD surfaces is X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) which gives comprehensive data on the oxidation states and different types of chemical bonds that are present, XPS aids in the understanding of the surface chemistry of CDs and the ways in which it can be altered to improve their functionality. This method is very helpful for examining how surface functionalization affects the optical characteristics of CDs, which enables the creation of CDs with specific features [66,67].

The stability of CDs is essential for their wide-ranging applications, including bioimaging, drug delivery, environmental monitoring, and light-emitting devices. Key aspects of CD stability include photostability, thermal stability, ion and pH resilience, time stability, chemical stability, and solvent compatibility.

Photostability: The capacity of CDs to maintain fluorescence after extended exposure to light, especially ultraviolet (UV) radiation, is known as photostability. Compared to CDs made of silica, research shows that embedding CDs in polymer matrices or salt crystals greatly increases their photostability. For example, CDs embedded in sodium chloride (NaCl) retain a larger proportion of their photoluminescence after prolonged UV exposure, but their silica-based equivalents often break down more quickly. The protective properties of salts or polymers, which shelter the CDs from damaging UV light, are primarily responsible for this enhancement [68–72].

Thermal stability: Another important consideration is thermal stability, particularly given that many CD applications need exposure to high temperatures. The synthesis techniques and encapsulating materials utilized affect the CDs heat stability. Even after hours at about 80 °C, CDs embedded in salts like potassium bromide (KBr) or NaCl show superior resistance to heat degradation, maintaining a substantial amount of their fluorescence intensity. On the other hand, CDs with silica matrices frequently see a sharp drop in fluorescence when heated. But it's crucial to remember that most CDs will ultimately deteriorate after extended heat exposure, underscoring the necessity of choosing materials carefully to guarantee lifetime [71–73].

Ion and pH stability: In general, CDs show good resistance to changes in pH and ionic conditions. They are appropriate for biological systems and environmental sensing where ionic conditions may change since studies have demonstrated that they can sustain fluorescence in high-salt environments including NaCl or other common cations. Furthermore, CDs show excellent pH stability in neutral to slightly acidic environments; nevertheless, diminished fluorescence may result from extremely high or low pH values. The efficacy of CDs in chemical sensing and biological applications depends on their adaptability.

Time stability: The capacity of CDs to maintain their characteristics over long stretches of time is referred to as time stability. According to several studies, when CDs are maintained normally, they can continue to exhibit fluorescence for several months. For long-term uses like electrical devices, bioimaging, and solid-state lighting, this extended stability is beneficial. CDs used in bioimaging, for instance, have proven to work consistently over time, which is essential for accurate diagnostic imaging.

Chemical stability: The carbon-rich structure of CDs gives them remarkable chemical stability and a high level of resistance to oxidation, which is a crucial property for long-term performance in oxidative conditions. When exposed to air, heat, or reactive chemicals, CDs can preserve their structural and functional integrity thanks to their oxidation resistance. Additionally, CDs exhibit resistance to deterioration in both basic and acidic conditions, maintaining their fluorescence characteristics. They are appropriate for demanding applications like catalysis and sensors because of their chemical stability.

Toxicity and biocompatibility: CDs made with non-toxic or natural raw materials usually have great biocompatibility and low toxicity. According to toxicity studies, CDs are non-lethal in animal models including C. elegans and mice at high doses and preserve over 85 % cell viability across a variety of cell lines, including HeLa, HepG2, and MCF-7. Environmentally friendly synthesis techniques that produce harmful chemicals are responsible for this low toxicity. Although surface functionalization and doping can improve biocompatibility even further, more research is required to evaluate long-term impacts and guarantee appropriateness for biomedical applications [74].

Fluorescence change can be used for sensing applications involving the concentration of different analytes. This phenomenon can be expressed as changes in intensity, wavelength, anisotropy, or fluorescence lifetime. The fluorescence changes observed in CDs are caused by a number of processes, all of which are being effectively utilized in sensing technologies [75,76].

Static quenching is a mechanism that reduces the overall intensity of fluorescent light by forming a non-fluorescent complex between the fluorescent molecule and the quencher. This process decreases the number of fluorescent molecules available for emission, leading to diminished fluorescence intensity ( Fig. 4a). Notably, during static quenching, there is no alteration in fluorescence lifetime. The interaction between the CD and the quencher results in the formation of non-fluorescent ground-state complexes. Key characteristics of static quenching include an unaltered fluorescence duration, reduced absorbance of fluorophores, and a diminishing quenching effect as temperature increases [77,78].

Dynamic quenching involves mechanisms such as charge or energy transfer, where collisions between the excited fluorescent molecule and the quencher facilitate a return to the ground state. For dynamic quenching to occur, the excited state must be in close proximity to the quencher, leading to a chemical reaction that accelerates the decay of the excited state. This results in a shortened fluorescence lifetime without affecting the absorption spectrum (Fig. 4b). Notable properties of dynamic quenching include a longer fluorescence lifetime, constant light absorption, and an increased quenching effect with rising temperature [79,80].

An oxidation-reduction process that involves transfer of electrons from one system to another is called PET (Fig. 4c). An internal redox interaction between the excited state of the fluorophore and another species that can either donate or take an electron characterizes PET, a deactivation mechanism. An elaborate complex is formed in PET between the electron acceptor and the source of electrons. This complex can return to the ground state without releasing any photons, though exciplex emission can occasionally be seen. The reductive electron transfer is controlled by the energy differential between the LUMO (lowest unoccupied molecular orbital) of quenchers and the HOMO (highest occupied molecular orbital) of CQDs. The energy differential between the CQDs LUMO and the quencher's LUMO governs oxidative electron transport. At the end of the procedure, the extra electron that was on the acceptor is transferred back to the electron donor [79].

For fluorescence sensors, resonance energy transfer is an additional useful feature [81,82]. During FRET, an initially excited molecule (donor) transfers energy to excite an electron on the acceptor while simultaneously returning to its ground state orbital. The process involves non-radiative energy transfer between donor (CDs fluorophore) and acceptor molecule (metal ion or analyte) also known as FRET pairs that takes place when the quencher's absorption spectra and CDs emission spectrum overlap. Due to long-range dipole-dipole interactions between CDs and the quencher, FRET proceeds without photon emission. Among the factors influencing the efficiency of energy transfer are the extent of overlap between the absorption spectrum of the acceptor and the fluorescence emission spectrum of the donor, as well as the spatial distance between them. When the distance is less than 10 nm and the overlap between the acceptor’s absorption spectra and the donor's emission spectrum is greater than 30 %, FRET is observed. Sensitive sensing applications are made possible by the ability to detect changes in the presence or concentration of the acceptor molecule by tracking changes in the FL intensity or lifetime of the donor CD. Because of their strong photostability, biocompatibility, and flexible fluorescence characteristics, FRET with CDs are preferred because they are useful in instruments for a variety of sensing platforms as depicted in Fig. 4d [83].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Heavy metal ion detection of carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Metal ions like copper, iron, zinc, and aluminum are vital nutrients that support life in a variety of organisms. However, these elements must be present within defined limitations; surpassing these thresholds can pose substantial dangers to human health and the environment. Furthermore, industrial processes often lead to the release of hazardous metal ions like cadmium (Cd²⁺), lead (Pb²⁺), and mercury (Hg²⁺) into ecosystems. Since, these heavy metal ions are non-biodegradable and may build up in living things, causing serious health problems such neurological illnesses, organ damage, and even death. Therefore, detecting these ions is essential for environmental and health risks [84,85]. For the purposes of environmental management and safety, it is essential that these ions be found early in biological, soil, or water samples [86]. It is necessary to design eco-friendly, effective sensors to mitigate the negative impacts of heavy metals. Traditional detection methods such as chromatography and mass spectrometry have limitations, including high operating and maintenance costs, the need for specialized personnel, and lack of portability. This has led to a growing interest in fluorescence sensors, particularly those that provide immediate and visible results.

Carbon dots have gained popularity as sensors due to their simple functionalization, chemical stability, and inherent fluorescent properties. CDs are chemically inert and have minimal cytotoxicity; they are often manufactured from natural precursors. Their many functional groups allow for easy surface modification. When interacting with heavy metal ions, CDs can exhibit variations in fluorescence, such as amplification or quenching, which makes them useful for ion detection and measurement. The oxygen-containing groups on the surface of CDs make it easier for metal ions to interact with them, which increases their usefulness as sensors. Utilizing surface functionalization strategies, CDs can get enhanced metal ion selectivity including iron, arsenic, lead, chromium, aluminium, cobalt etc., The main uses of heavy metal ion detection are in environmental monitoring, especially with relation to water quality, industrial safety evaluations, and clinical diagnostics. This section will explore several types of plant precursor used for CDs synthesis and utilized for various metal ions sensing application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Plant-derived carbon dots for ferric and ferrous ion detection
ce:section.ce:para: Iron is an essential metal ion in biological systems, primarily existing in two oxidation states: ferric (Fe³⁺) and ferrous (Fe²⁺). Among these, the ferric state is more toxic due to its insolubility, which can lead to the formation of harmful radicals. It is essential for several physiological functions, such as the synthesis of DNA, the transfer of oxygen, and the creation of energy. However, because Fe³ ⁺ tends to produce reactive oxygen species, which can harm tissues and cells, an excess of Fe³ ⁺can be harmful. An abnormal presence of Fe³ ⁺ is associated with serious health issues, including anemia, heart failure, cognitive decline, and diabetes. According to World Health Organization (WHO) standards, the maximum acceptable limit of Fe(III) in drinking water is 0.3 (mg/L) [87,88]. Therefore, monitoring the concentration of Fe³ ⁺ in both environmental and biological systems is crucial. Aquatic ecosystems and water quality can be impacted by iron. Sedimentation and toxic algal blooms are two problems that may be prevented and addressed with the use of iron level monitoring in water bodies. The various plants parts used for CDs synthesis ( Fig. 5) and the use of CDs iron ion detection and corresponding sensing data are tabulated in Table 2.

CDs derived from various plant sources have shown significant potential for sensing iron ions, particularly Fe³⁺ and Fe²⁺. For instance, CDs synthesized from the pseudo-stem of banana plants (SSB-CDs) using a simple hydrothermal method ( Fig. 6a). These SSB-CDs achieved a QY of 48 % and displayed a strong fluorescence “turn-off” response to Fe³⁺ ions, with a detection limit of 6.4 nM. The SSB-CDs showed absorption peak at 284 nm corresponds to π– π* attributed to the CC bonds (Fig. 6b). Additionally, their fluorescence spectra revealed multi emission property under different excitation wavelength (Fig. 6c). This sensitivity is attributed to strong interactions between Fe³⁺ ions and functional groups on the SSB-CDs surface, leading to significant FL quenching[89]. Similarly, CDs synthesized using the fruit extract of Chionanthus retusus yielded nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs) with high sensitivity towards Fe³⁺ ions and a low detection limit of 70 μM. These N-CDs exhibited notable FL quenching in the presence of Fe³⁺ ions, making them suitable for metal ion sensing applications. Another natural source, mango peel, was utilized to synthesize fluorescent CDs, which showed effective FL quenching towards Fe²⁺ ions. These CDs could detect Fe²⁺ concentrations in the range of 4 μM to 16 μM, with a detection limit of 1.2 μM. Their selectivity for Fe²⁺ is significant, as minimal quenching occurs with other metal ions, enhancing their utility in analytical applications [90].

In addition, N-CDs derived from Chionanthus retusus fruit extract exhibited high biocompatibility and water solubility. For Fe³⁺ sensing, these CDs achieved a detection limit of 70 μM[92]. Another example involves N-CDs synthesized from Magnolia liliiflora, which appeared nearly spherical with an average size of 4 ± 1 nm, as observed via TEM. These CDs demonstrated a QY of approximately 11 %, showcasing strong fluorescence properties. For Fe³⁺ detection, these CDs exhibited a detection limit of 1.2 μM, effective for identifying low concentrations of Fe³⁺ ions in solutions. The photoluminescence properties of N-CDs are illustrated in Fig. 6d, the FL quenching efficiency with different metal ions (at 1 mM concentration) is shown in Fig. 6e. The FL intensity ratios of CDs with various metal ions (Fig. 6f) and their application on handmade filter paper under UV light (Fig. 6g) further highlight their utility[91]. The CDs synthesized from coffee beans via a green, one-pot hydrothermal method demonstrated detection limits of 15.4 nM for down-conversion fluorescence and 16.3 nM for up-conversion fluorescence assays. These CDs exhibited a QY of 9.8 % when excited at 360 nm with an average size of 4.6 nm, with a narrow size distribution [93].

Natural sources rich in carbon, nitrogen, and sulfur, such as garlic, have also been utilized to synthesize N-S-doped CDs (N-S-CDs). These CDs exhibit strong fluorescence quenching upon interaction with Fe³⁺ ions, primarily due to the robust coordination between Fe³⁺ ions and functional groups like hydroxyl groups on the CD surface. This quenching effect allows for the selective and sensitive detection of Fe³⁺, with LOD as low as 0.2 µM [94]. Another noteworthy example involves CDs synthesized from sweet potatoes through hydrothermal treatment. These CDs demonstrate FL quenching in the presence of Fe³⁺ ions, enabling selective detection with an LOD of 0.32 μM. The biocompatibility of these CDs makes them ideal for applications in biological imaging and monitoring iron levels in living cells [95]. Similarly, CDs prepared from pineapple (Ananas comosus) via an acid oxidation method ( Fig. 7a) exhibited exceptional sensitivity for Fe³ ⁺ detection. The FL quenching effect in these CDs is attributed to the formation of complexes between Fe³⁺ ions and surface groups on the CDs, resulting in a highly sensitive detection limit of 30 nM and a linear detection range from 0.05 to 500 µM [96]. The use of banana peels as a bio-resource for synthesizing CQDs highlights their versatility. The CQDs derived from different types of banana peels A-CQDs (normal yellow banana), B-CQDs (small yellow banana), C-CQDs (green banana), and D-CQDs (red banana) demonstrated varying LODs for Fe³⁺ detection: 6.1 μM, 7.3 μM, 7.1 μM, and 11.3 μM, respectively. Fig. 7f visually represents the FL quenching of these A-CQDs upon exposure to Fe³⁺ ions under 360 nm UV light and natural daylight. The average sizes of these CQDs, as observed via TEM analysis (Fig. 7b–e), were 3.49 nm for A-CQDs, 5.1 nm for B-CQDs, 5.25 nm for C-CQDs, and 4.24 nm for D-CQDs. The quenching mechanism of these CDs is attributed to the presence of functional groups such as -OH, -CO, and -COOH on their surfaces (Fig. 7g). Among the CQDs, A-CQDs showed the highest QY of 18.06 %, followed by C-CQDs (13.06 %), B-CQDs (7.72 %), and D-CQDs (2.6 %). TEM analysis revealed that the CQDs synthesized from banana peels had sizes ranging from 4 to 9 nm. These findings highlight the potential of banana-derived CQDs for fluorescence-based Fe³⁺ sensing, further establishing their utility in environmental and biological applications [97].

The synthesis of fluorescent CQDs from Tagetes patula (TP-CQDs) flowers displayed a QY of 29.88 % and an average particle size of 5.15 nm, as determined by TEM. The TP-CQDs exhibited exceptional sensitivity for Fe³⁺ detection, with a LOD of 0.32 μM. This eco-friendly and efficient method highlights the potential of TP-CQDs for iron sensing applications. The mechanism of FL quenching for these CQDs is illustrated in Fig. 7h, while their stability and photostability were evaluated by analyzing FL emission over time (Fig. 7i) and under UV light exposure (365 nm) for 90 min (Fig. 7j) [98]. Biomass sources such as pear juice have also been utilized to synthesize CQDs via a one-step hydrothermal process. These CQDs exhibit strong optical properties and biocompatibility, making them suitable for diverse applications. The LOD for Fe³⁺ detection was approximately 2.28 µM, and for ascorbic acid (AA), it was 1.27 µM. The CQDs had a QY of 18 %, with sizes ranging between 3–6 nm. High-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images revealed spherical morphology, graphitic spacing, and a size distribution histogram ( Fig. 8a–c). Spectral analyses, including FTIR and XPS, highlighted functional groups and elemental composition, as shown in Fig. 8d–f [99]. The N-CQDs were prepared from biomass waste, such as banana petioles, through a hydrothermal carbonization process (Fig. 8g). This method is environmentally friendly and does not require additional chemicals, promoting sustainability and waste valorization. The FL intensity of NCDs with different metal ions were performed, the maximum quenching of FL intensity was observed for Fe³⁺ ions. The fluorescence and absorbance spectra of NCDs was shown in Fig. 8h and i. Fluorescent titration of NCDs were performed with varying concentration of FeCl3, quenching of FL intensity was observed with increasing the concentration of Fe³⁺. The fluorescent and absorption spectra of NCDs with different concentration of Fe³⁺ ions were illustrated in Fig. 8j and k. The LOD for NCQDs in sensing Fe³⁺ ions are notably low. Recent studies reported that LOD as low as 0.21 nM, which is significantly lower than many traditional methods. Fig. 8l represents the smartphone-paper based colorimetric sensor, change in the color of the NCDs were observed only for Fe³⁺ ions not for any other metal ions which shows the selectivity. The LOD values are 0.47 nM, for solution-based assays and 0.94 nM for paper-based assays and the size of N-CQDs typically ranges from 2 to 10 nm [100].

Cranberry beans were utilized for the synthesis of CDs (CB-CDs) by hydrothermal method as shown in Fig. 9a. These biomass-derived CDs exhibit strong fluorescence properties with the LOD for Fe³⁺ ions are around 9.55 µM. Most of the CB-CDs excited at 380 nm, the FL intensity of CDs at different excitation wavelength and the normalized fluorescent intensity was shown in Fig. 9b and c. The FL quenching of the CB-CDs may be due to the aggregation of the CB-CDs after the addition of Fe³⁺ ions. The FL quenching mechanism and aggregation of CDs was given in Fig. 9d and e. The QY of these cranberry bean-derived CB-CDs is approximately 10.85 %. TEM analysis reveals that the CDs have a spherical shape with an average size of 3.96 nm [101].

The fluorescent CDs were synthesized from Boswellia ovalifoliolata bark extract using a hydrothermal method. These CDs demonstrated a high sensitivity for Fe³⁺ ions with a LOD of 0.41 μM and the QY of the CDs was approximately 10.2 %. The TEM and SEM images indicates that CDs are spherical in shape without any aggregation ( Fig. 10a and b). The TEM images indicated an average particle size of about 4.16 ± 0.22 nm. The XRD pattern of CDs shows the presence of graphitic carbon (Fig. 10c). The raman spectrum shows the presence of two peaks which corresponds to D band and G band (Fig. 10d). The fluorescent lifetime of CDs was carried out in the presence and absence of Fe³⁺ ions as shown in Fig. 10e [103]. The CDs were synthesized from biomass, such as watermelon juice, using a green hydrothermal process. The N-CDs exhibited temperature dependent fluorescence behaviour, on increasing the temperature (1–98 °C) there is a decline in the FL emission was observed Fig. 10f. The photographs of the N-CDs dispersed at 10 °C and 50 °C under 365 nm UV light are shown in Fig. 10g. The CDs exhibited a stable fluorescence at corresponding temperature, it can act as a temperature nano sensor with high reproducibility and accuracy (Fig. 10h). These N-CQDs have a LOD of 0.16 µM and QY of the synthesized N-CQDs is around 10.6 %. These dots have a spherical shape with sizes ranging from 3 to 7 nm[104]. Similarly, mangosteen pulp-derived CDs, synthesized via hydrothermal methods, can detect Fe³⁺ ions at concentrations as low as 52 nM. This CDs fluorescence lifetimes decreasing from 5.71 ns to 4.89 ns in the presence of Fe³⁺, indicating dynamic quenching due to electron transfer. The schematic representation of CDs synthesized from mangosteen pulp for metal ion detection and cell imaging was given in Fig. 10i. The TEM image shows that CDs exhibit high dispersity and uniform spherical shape (Fig. 10j) with aggregation morphology of about 25 nm in diameter (Fig. 10k) [4]. CDs synthesized from rice straw using a microwave-hydrothermal method possess spherical morphology and a QY of up to 22.58 %, demonstrating sizes ranging from 2.10 to 3.39 nm. Additionally, CDs derived from mango peel have emerged as effective fluorescent probes for Fe²⁺ detection, exhibiting significant quenching within a linear range of 4–16 μM and a LOD of 1.2 μM, with minimal interference from other metal ions [105].

CDs from coriander leaves were utilized for the Fe³⁺ ions sensing by fluorescence quenching. Additionally, they evaluated the biocompatibility and bioimaging capabilities of the CDs on both normal lung cells (L-132) and cancer cells (A549). Furthermore, the antioxidant activity of the CDs was investigated, validating their practicality as a versatile tool derived from a herbal carbon source for various applications in environmental sensing and biomedical fields [106]. A simple and environmentally friendly process for synthesizing luminescent CDs by using rose-heart radish. The resultant CDs have a high FL QY of 13.6 %, good chemical stability, minimal toxicity, and outstanding biocompatibility, among other benefits. Significant FL quenching results from the N-CDs shows strong sensitivity towards Fe2+ ions. With a linear detection range of 0.02–40 μM and a LOD of 0.13 μM, this feature has been used to develop a straightforward fluorescent technique for detecting Fe³⁺ ions [117]. For the first time, cellulose-rich durian shell waste (DSW) was used in a one-pot method to synthesis CDs without the use of any modifying agents.

The blue-green emitting CDs showed stability and resilience to photobleaching throughout a broad pH range and chloride concentrations. Through the formation of a non-fluorescent ground complex with oxygen functional groups, they exhibited remarkable selectivity towards Fe³⁺ ions. The detection limit of 128 nM was possible throughout a range of 0–20 μM according to a linear connection between normalized FL intensity and Fe³⁺ concentration [118]. A straightforward one-step pyrolysis process was used to produce CDs using Aloe vera extract. The resultant CDs had a QY of 12.3 % and shows intense blue luminescence when exposed to UV light. These CDs also showed light-activated antibacterial activity against Staphylococcus aureus and E. coli, and were tested for Fe(III) ion sensing in water without surface changes [119]. The hydrothermal treatment of natural Siberian elm (Ulmus pumila L.) seeds served as a carbon source for the synthesis of CDs. The CDs potential for application as Fe³⁺ sensors was indicated by the TEM images, which showed that they were approximately spherical with an average diameter of 3.83 nm and they selectively quenched fluorescence in the presence of Fe³⁺ ions. The degree of quenching was linearly associated with the concentration of Fe³⁺. Biocompatibility was confirmed through MTT assays, and the CDs effectively labeled as HepG2 cells with bright blue fluorescence[107]. The N-CDs were synthesized from black soya beans using a one-step pyrolysis method with the detection limit of 96.87 nM and linear range of 0.2–300 μM. The QY of the N-CDs was measured to be 38.7 ± 0.64 % and an average size of 5.16 nm[108]. The N-CDs synthesized from betel leaves using a green hydrothermal method. The detection limit for Fe³⁺ was 0.135 μM in a linear range of 0.3–3.3 μM [109].

The Snake gourd peel extract was utilized for synthesis of CDs and the limit of detection for Fe³⁺ ions was determined to be 0.398 µM[110]. The CQDs were synthesized from green pomelo peel and its showed detection limit towards Fe³⁺ ions (0.086 µM). The CQDs had a QY of 17.31 %, indicating decent fluorescence efficiency. The TEM analysis showed that the CQDs had an average particle size of approximately 5.5 nm, with a size range of 1.2–8.4 nm[111]. The CDs were developed from Volvariella volvacea mushroom via a hydrothermal method and the detection limit for Fe³⁺ ions were 16 nM, indicating a highly sensitive detection capability. The CDs exhibited a QY of 11.5 %, the average particle size of the CDs was about 5.8 nm, with sizes ranging from 3 to 8 nm[112]. The CDs were derived from Miscanthus grass a sustainable and cost-effective biomass source to detect Fe³⁺ ions. The process involves a green, one-step heating method, making it environmentally friendly. The N-doped Miscanthus-derived CDs show excellent sensitivity, with a LOD of 20 nM for Fe³⁺ ions and the N-doped CDs have a high QY of 11.65 %, indicating good efficiency in converting absorbed light into emitted fluorescence. The average size of the N-doped Miscanthus-derived CDs is around 4.6 ± 0.21 nm. The mango peel-derived CDs shows LOD of 1.2 μM for Fe²⁺ ions and the QY is 8.5 %. The size of the mango peel-derived CDs ranges from 2 to 6 nm with an average size of 3 nm [113].

The carbon dots (Tea-CDs) were synthesized from waste tea using a green hydrothermal method. Waste tea extract was used as a carbon source, making the synthesis process eco-friendly and cost-effective. The Tea-CDs were highly sensitive for Fe³⁺ detection, with a LOD of 0.15 μM. The QY of the Tea-CDs was measured to be approximately 7.1 %. Tea-CDs were spherical and had an average particle size of about 10 nm [114]. The CQDs used for sensing iron were synthesized from the endosperm of Borassus flabellifer, commonly known as ice apple through a simple hydrothermal method. The synthesized CQDs exhibited a LOD for Fe³⁺ ions of 2.01 μM. The QY achieved was 0.54, which is considered significant for fluorescent materials [115]. Piper betle (betel leaves) were utilized for the synthesis of the CQDs and its showed a LOD for Fe³⁺ ions of 50 nM. TEM analysis revealed that the size of the synthesized CQDs ranged from 3 to 7 nm [116]. The CDs used for iron sensing were synthesized from Lycii Fructus (the dried ripe fruits of Lycium chinense) through a green hydrothermal method. CDs exhibited a LOD for Fe³⁺ of 21 nM and the QY of the synthesized CDs was measured at 17.2 %. The size of the CDs ranged from 2 to 5 nm, with an average diameter of approximately 3.3 nm [117]. The red lentils as carbon source used for CDs synthesis by hydrothermal method. The developed CDs demonstrated a LOD of 0.10 μM for Fe³⁺ ions, within a dynamic range of 2–20 μM. The average size of the CQDs was approximately 6 nm, with most particles ranging between 4–10 nm [118].

CDs were derived from Malus floribunda fruit extract using a hydrothermal method. The detection limit for Fe³⁺ ions using N-CDs was found to be 0.10 μM with a QY of 19 %. The N-CDs have an average size of about 4.0 nm, with a narrow size distribution [119]. The hydrophilic carbon dots (M-CDs) were synthesized using Morus nigra fruit as the carbon source. The M-CDs demonstrated a detection limit of 0.47 μM for Fe³⁺ ions, with a linear detection range between 5 and 30 µM. The synthesized M-CDs exhibited a high QY of 24 % and the M-CDs have a mean diameter of approximately 4.5 nm [120]. N-CQDs were developed using wolfberry as the carbon source by one-step hydrothermal method. The N-CQDs exhibited a detection limit of 3 μmol/L for Fe³⁺ ions and a high QY of 22 % [121]. The CQDs were derived from cabbage, utilizing a green and facile one-step carbonization process. CQDs demonstrated a detection limit for Fe³⁺ ions at 3 μmol/L, indicating their effectiveness for sensitive detection in various applications. The synthesized CQDs exhibited a QY of 16.5 % [122]. CQDs with blue fluorescent properties have been synthesized from coconut water using a simple, cost-effective method. The CQDs exhibited a detection limit of 0.30 μM for Fe³⁺ ions and the CQDs have an average grain size of approximately 5 nm, indicating their nanoscale dimension and uniformity [123]. Prunus cerasifera fruits were utilized as a carbon source to produce highly luminescent CDs for Fe³ ⁺ ions detection. The CDs demonstrated low toxicity towards HepG2 cells, exhibiting blue and green fluorescence during in vitro imaging [124]. The onion waste-used to derived for highly fluorescent CDs with a QY of 28 %. Cytotoxicity studies conducted on both normal HEK-293 cells and cancerous HeLa cells revealed excellent biocompatibility [125]. Blueberry were utilized for the synthesis of fluorescent CDs by a simple method involving centrifugation and liquid nitrogen treatment. Various conditions like concentration, temperature, pH, and excitation wavelength, fluorescence, and time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopies were investigated for the study of photophysical property of CDs [126]. The CQDs were derived from various biomass wastes, including orange peel, Ginkgo biloba leaves, paulownia leaves, and magnolia flowers, through a facile hydrothermal process. The synthesized CQDs demonstrated a detection limit of 0.073 μM for Fe³⁺ ions, with a sensitive response observed in the concentration range of 0.2–100 μM[127]. The N-CD has been synthesized using Morinda coreia fruits as a green biomass resource. The N-CDs exhibited a detection limit of 1.32 μM for Fe³ ⁺ ions and the QY of the synthesized N-CDs was calculated to be 17.1 % [128]. The highly FL CQDs were synthesized using mango (Mangifera indica) leaves through an inexpensive, green, and one-pot pyrolysis method at 300 °C for 3 h. The synthesized CQDs exhibited a detection limit of 0.62 ppm for Fe²⁺ ions. The CQDs were spherical in shape with an average size of less than 10 nm, indicating their nanoscale dimensions which are beneficial for their optical properties [129].

The CDs were derived from tuberose (Polianthes tuberose L.) petals had an average size of approximately 4 nm and QY of 3 % [130]. The N-CDs were synthesized from the waste biomass of Poa pratensis (Kentucky bluegrass), and it exhibit a detection limit of 1.4 μM for Fe³⁺ ions and the QY of 7 % [131]. The synthesize of CQDs from Catharanthus roseus (white) leaves through a hydrothermal method for multi-ion sensing and bioimaging applications. The CDs exhibited a LOD of 0.3 μM for Fe³⁺ ions within a linear range of 0–6 μM the synthesized CQDs demonstrated an impressive QY of 28.2 % [132].

Due to its superior electrical and thermal conductivity, malleability, and corrosion resistance, copper is a very useful and important metal. As a trace element, copper is essential for the functioning of the immune system, iron metabolism, and enzyme synthesis in living things. Over exposure to copper can lead to oxidative stress in cells, liver and kidney damage, neurological diseases, and contamination of the environment. A suggested limit of 2.0 mg/L (2000 µg/L) is set for copper. It’s crucial to regularly check the amount of copper in drinking water to guarantee both safety and adherence to regulations. In this section, we summarize plant-derived carbon dots as fluorescent probes for the detection of Cu²⁺ ions ( Table 3)

The Eleusine coracana (finger millet)-derived CDs used for Cu²⁺ ion detection. A linear relationship between quenching efficiency and Cu2+ concentration was established, with a detection limit as low as 10 nM. The synthesized CQDs demonstrated bright blue fluorescence under UV light and a significant quenching of FL intensity in the presence of Cu2+ compared to other metal ions. Due to its affordability, speed, and environmental friendliness, this approach is especially beneficial for the examination of real water samples [75]. The fluorescent CDs, which were developed from prawn shells with an average diameter of 4 nm, had a high QY of 9 %, strong monodispersity, good stability, great water solubility, and blue fluorescence under UV light (λ = 365 nm). The CDs exhibits high selectivity and sensitivity, together with their low detection limit of 5 nM, were investigated for their potential use as efficient sensing probes for Cu2+ detection. Cu2+ ions in seawater samples were effectively analyzed using this innovative sensor technology [209]. The raw material used to synthesis the nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped luminous CDs was lily bulbs (LB). With a QY of 17.6 %, the resultant LB-CDs showed intense fluorescence and great stability in aqueous conditions. The produced LB-CDs demonstrated exceptional sensitivity and selectivity for Cu2+ ions, with a linear detection range of 0.05–2.0 μM and a detection limit of 12.8 nM. The LB-CDs demonstrated low cytotoxicity and were effectively used for Cu2+ ion detection in real water samples, which qualified them for cell imaging and fluorescence-based analysis [206]. With a QY of 10.58 %, peanut shells were used to create CDs through a one-pot pyrolysis process that was optimized with a central composite experiment design. 70 g of peanut shell weight was recorded, and the ideal pyrolysis conditions were found to be 400 °C for 4 h. The resultant CDs have great photostability, good water dispersibility, and distinctive excitation-dependent behavior. After three to four hours at 400 °C, the peanut shells completely burned, according to FT-IR examination of the pyrolysis process. There were different amounts of carbon-rich residues left over after pyrolysis, the PL intensity of the CDs was not always proportionate to the corresponding QY value. Their sensitivity and selectivity in Cu2+ detection was assessed using fluorescence-quenching tests, which showed a detection limit of 4.8 mM [223]. A straightforward, inexpensive, and environmentally friendly hydrothermal process was used to create the CQDs utilizing bamboo leaves as a carbon source. These BPEI-CQDs were employed as fluorescent probes to detect Cu2+ ions with high sensitivity and selectivity. According to their experimental findings, the produced CQDs have a narrow size distribution and an average diameter of 3.6 nm. The high QY of 7.1 % was attained by the biomass-based CQDs. With a dynamic range of 0.33–66.6 μM and a limit of detection as low as 115 nM, the BPEI-CQDs-based sensing system offers a straightforward, dependable, and sensitive approach for Cu2+ detection [204]. The successful production of fluorescent CDs from microbial fermented Pu-erh tea using a simple brewing method ( Fig. 11a), which yields CDs with abundant surface functional groups. The fluorescence response of CDs in different metal ions was given in Fig. 11b. The detection of metal ions like Cu²⁺ and Fe²⁺ and the LOD for these ions using CDs has been reported to be as low as 0.17 µM for Fe³⁺, 0.31 µM for Fe²⁺, and 0.62 µM for Cu²⁺. Fluorescence colours with different concentration of copper under 365 nm in UV light given in Fig. 11c. FL spectra of CDs in different concentration of Cu²⁺ and the plot of log I/I0 as a function of copper concentration was given in Fig. 11d and e. The TEM images typically reveal that CDs are small, often ranging from 2 to 10 nm. CDs sourced from natural materials like Pu-erh tea show promising sensing applications due to their favourable properties, including low limits of detection for metal ions, high QYs, and nanoscale sizes observed through TEM analysis [202].

The synthesis of CDs from tender coconut water, demonstrating a simple and effective green synthesis approach Fig. 11f. In the context of detecting thiamine (vitamin B1), the LOD was found to be 280 nM when using CDs modified with Cu²⁺ ions and the fluorescence response was given Fig. 11g. The FL imaging of CDs, CDs+Cu2+ and CDs+Cu2++thiamine was given in the Fig. 11h-j. The CDs are nanoscale materials, with sizes ranging from 2 ± 0.5 (140 °C), 4 ± 1 (160 °C), and 4 ± 2 nm (180 °C)[209]. The synthesized CQDs were derived from Rambutan and Pandan leaves using hydrothermal and microwave methods. These CQDs display strong fluorescence and are used for sensing of Cu²⁺ ions due to their interaction with functional groups like hydroxyl and carbonyl on the CQDs surface. The lowest detection limit for Cu²⁺ ions is observed in CQDs synthesized from dried Rambutan leaves via the hydrothermal method, with a LOD of 123.67 µM. The CQDs exhibit QY ranging from 0.87 % to 2.79 %, with the highest yield observed in CQDs derived from dried Pandan leaves using the hydrothermal method[205].

The grape seeds were utilized for the synthesis of nitrogen and sulfur doped CDs (N, S-CDs). The detection limit for Cu²⁺ ions using these CQDs is approximately 0.048 mg/L, indicating high sensitivity. The CQDs exhibit a fluorescence QY of about 27.5 %, showing strong fluorescence efficiency due to the nitrogen and sulfur co-doping. The TEM analysis shows that the CQDs are uniformly spherical with an average diameter of 8.9 nm [206]. Crown daisy leaves, a type of kitchen waste, were used for synthesize of N-CDs. This eco-friendly approach reuses organic waste and the fluorescent probe made from these N-CDs exhibited a detection limit for Cu²⁺ ions as low as 1.0 nM. TEM showed that the N-CDs had well-dispersed particles with diameters ranging from 5.0 to 10.0 nm [204].

Lead has been utilized for millennia in a variety of sectors, lead (Pb) is a heavy metal that is very harmful to both humans and the environment. It is also recognized to have no good effects on biological systems [210]. Lead exposure, even at low concentrations, can cause major health issues, particularly in children, such as cognitive decline, developmental delays, and liver, kidney, and brain damage. Lead in drinking water might have a maximum allowable value is less than 15.0 ppb (72 nM) set by Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) [211]. For the protection of public health and environmental monitoring, lead detection must be done effectively. In this section, we summarize plant-derived carbon dots as fluorescent probes for the detection of Pb²⁺ ions ( Table 4). The highly fluorescent NCDs were synthesized using Lantana camara berries. The NCDs demonstrated bright fluorescence with a QY of 33.15 %, showing stability under conditions such as heat, pH variations, ionic strength, and continuous irradiation. Additionally, the NCDs exhibited a highly selective and sensitive fluorescence response to Pb2+, making them a promising label-free fluorescent probe for effective Pb2+ detection. The NCD-based probe displayed a strong linear response to Pb2+ concentrations ranging from 0 to 200 nM, with a LOD of 9.64 nM [212]. Similarly, another N-CDs synthesized from Lantana camara berries, which serve as a carbon source. The synthesis involves a hydrothermal process using ethylene diamine as the nitrogen source [213]. Carbon dots were synthesized from oyster mushrooms (Pleurotus species), using hydrothermal carbonization process and its used for biological application ( Fig. 12 a). These CDs demonstrated an LOD of 58.63 μM for detecting lead ions (Pb²⁺) when used as a colorimetric sensor. TEM analysis reveals that the size of CDs can vary significantly based on their synthesis method. For example, those derived from oyster mushrooms typically exhibit sizes ranging from 3 to 8 nm [214].

The NCDs derived from Lantana camara exhibited a remarkable LOD of 9.64 nM when used as a fluorescent probe for lead ion detection. The NCDs demonstrated a high QY of 33.15 %, which is stable under various conditions such as heat and pH changes. This high QY enhances their utility in fluorescence-based applications like sensing and imaging. TEM analysis revealed that the synthesized NCDs possess a near-spherical morphology with sizes ranging from 3 to 8 nm, averaging around 5 ± 3 nm. These CDs with MCF cells pre-treated with 10 μM Pb²⁺ for 1 h showed very weak intracellular fluorescence under a confocal microscope (Fig. 12b), demonstrating the nanosensor’s effectiveness for intracellular Pb²⁺ detection [215]. The fluorescent CDs were developed using the leaves of Ocimum sanctum and its exhibit high stability in aqueous solutions and strong fluorescence with a QY of 9.3 %. These CDs were used for detecting Pb2+ ions, showed LOD of 0.59 nM and a linear detection range of 0.01–1.0 μM [216].

The table sugar-derived CDs achieves a remarkable detection limit of 14 ppb, which is permissible limit of 15 ppb for Pb(II) ions in drinking water [218]. The synthesis of CDs using potato dextrose agar (PDA) as the carbon source through microwave-assisted pyrolysis. The QY of these CDs was approximately 9 %, and TEM revealed an average particle size of around 4.3 nm. The CDs exhibited a highly sensitive detection limit for Pb²⁺ ions, with a LOD of 110 pM in solution and 106 pM on a paper-based sensor strip. The cellulosic filter papers were used to fabricate paper strips, which were exposed to an aqueous Pb²⁺ solution for 5 min. Upon exposure to Pb²⁺ ions, the FL intensity of the CDs decreased with increasing Pb²⁺ concentration, as shown in Fig. 12c, with a good linear correlation up to 1 μM (Fig. 12d). No color change was observed when the paper strips were exposed to other metal ions, indicating the selectivity of the CDs for Pb²⁺ ions (Fig. 12e). Furthermore, the addition of two fluorescent dyes, Rhodamine B (RhB) and Cyanine 5 (Cy5), into the CD solutions resulted in color changes, which reverted to their original color upon the introduction of Pb²⁺ ions (Fig. 12f, g).

Mercury (Hg) is a heavy metal that has been found in a variety of forms, such as organic mercury compounds (like methylmercury), inorganic mercury compounds, and pure mercury [226]. Though historically used in dental fillings, thermometers, batteries, and industrial processes, mercury presents serious risks to ecosystems and human health. Once released into the environment, mercury can convert into methylmercury, a highly toxic form that bioaccumulates in aquatic food chains, leading to severe health risks such as brain damage, kidney failure, and developmental issues, particularly in infants. Therefore, detection of mercury is very much essential. In recent years, CDs derived from plant-based precursors have gained attention as FL probes for the detection of mercury ions (Hg²⁺) due to their excellent sensitivity, selectivity, strong QY, and environmentally friendly production.

There have been reports of many plant-based CDs for Hg²⁺ ion detection each with unique characteristics (
Tables 5 and 6). Similarly, Pomelo peel-derived CDs synthesized using a hydrothermal method at 200 °C for 3 h demonstrated a QY of 6.9 % ( Fig. 13a). The resulting CDs were further utilized as probes for Hg2+ detection showed limit of detection as low as 0.23 nM [227]. Gardenia fruit-derived N/S-doped CDs were synthesized through hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C for 5 h (Fig. 13c). The fluorescence of these N/S-CDs can be quenched by Hg2+ ions (turn-off), and the quenched fluorescence can be restored (turn-on) by introducing cysteine (Cys [228]. Orange juice-derived nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs) were prepared through hydrothermal decomposition method, yielding mono-dispersed nanoparticles with sizes ranging from 0.5 to 3.0 nm. These N-CDs displayed good dispersion in aqueous solutions across a pH range of 3.0–13.0. The nitrogen doping enhanced their FL intensity, achieving a QY of 31.7 %. The FL intensity of these N-CDs increased proportionally with Hg²⁺ concentrations from 4.0 to 32.0 μg/mL, with excellent recovery (102–103 %) in spiked samples [229]. Another, nitrogen-doped fluorescent CDs with a nitrogen content of 5.23 % were developed from lotus root (LR), without the need for additional surface passivation agents. The resulting LR-CDs showed size of 9.41 nm and QY of 19 %. These LR-CDs demonstrated high selectivity for Hg²⁺ with an LOD of 18.7 nM and a linear detection range of 0.1–60.0 μM, making them suitable for environmental mercury sensing [230].

Hongcaitai-derived CDs, synthesized hydrothermally, exhibited an LOD of 60 nM for mercury ions when tested in river water, with particle sizes ranging from 1.9 to 2.6 nm. Two variants, CDs-A (ethanol-soluble) and CDs-B (ethanol-insoluble), displayed QYs of 17.8 % and 12.1 %, respectively [104]. Similarly, pineapple peel-derived CDs, composed mainly of cellulose, hemicellulose, lignin, and pectin, achieved a QY of 42 % and an LOD of 4.5 nM, with particle sizes between 2 and 3 nm. The study highlights these CDs potential not only for mercury sensing but also for applications in molecular keypad locks and memory devices [231]. Prosopis juliflora leaf-derived CDs exhibited an average size of 5.8 nm, a QY of 5 %, and a LOD of 1.26 ng/mL (6.3 nM) [48]. Tamarindus indica leaf-based CDs, synthesized through a green hydrothermal method, exhibited exceptional optical properties with a high QY of 46.6 % and an LOD of 6 nM. TEM images showed an average size of 3.4 ± 0.5 nm. These CDs offered a linear detection range between 0 and 0.1 μM, indicating their effectiveness for mercury detection at low concentrations [104,232]. The CDs were synthesized from Jinhua bergamot, a citrus fruit showing QY approximately 50.78 %, indicating a strong fluorescence. The limit of detection for mercury ions (Hg²⁺) using these CDs is reported to be 5.5 nM [147]. The study on mercury sensing using nitrogen and phosphorus dual-doped carbon dots (A-NPCDs) derived from Dunaliella salina [233]. Additional sources include sugarcane waste-derived CDs, with an LOD of 0.1 μM for mercury ions and applications in bioimaging, and Curcuma amada-based CDs, which achieved an LOD of 0.2 nM for dual sensing applications. China grass carp-derived microwave-synthesized CDs exhibited an LOD of 0.014 mmol/L, while cucumber juice-based CDs showed an LOD of 180 nM, confirming the versatility of plant-based precursors. Corn bract leaf-derived CDs and muskmelon-based CDs also demonstrated effective mercury detection with LODs of 9 nM and 330 nM, respectively, further expanding the range of natural carbon sources for fluorescence-based sensing. These attributes highlight the potential of A-NPCDs as effective and eco-friendly sensors for mercury detection in various applications [234].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of other metal ion using plant-derived carbon dots as fluorescent probe

ce:section.ce:para: The most common metallic element in the Earth crust, aluminium is a silvery-white, light metal that makes up around 8 % of its total weight. Due to natural or industrial processes, aluminium may contaminate water supplies and pose a health concern. For the sake of public health, its content in drinking water and natural bodies must be regularly monitored. Excessive exposure to aluminium has been linked to a number of health concerns, including neurological disorders. According to WHO, the permissible limit of Al3+ is ∼3–10 mg daily and 7 mg/kg weekly (depending on body weight)[251]. Sensing devices are essential for monitoring aluminium dust or other airborne particles and ensuring worker safety.

Carbon dots derived from Pyrus pyrifolia (pear) fruit were used for the synthesis of fluorescent CDs exhibit intense blue fluorescence and are highly selective for detecting aluminium ions (Al3+) due to specific surface functional groups. The detection mechanism relies on CHEF, where the FL intensity increases with higher concentrations of Al3+. The CDs can detect Al3+ at concentrations as low as 2.5 nM, making them highly sensitive. The nanoprobe was successfully tested on spiked water samples, proving its effectiveness for real-world aluminium detection. Additionally, these CDs were demonstrated to have potential in bioimaging, as they were used to image Bacillus subtilis bacterial cells, suggesting broader applications in cell imaging [252]. Fluorescent N-CQDs were synthesized by using Osmanthus fragrans exhibit a high QY of 21.9 %, with strong blue fluorescence and excellent water dispersibility. The sensing mechanism involves the IFE between N-CQDs and quercetin (QT), which causes FL quenching. Upon the addition of aluminium ions (Al3+), the specific binding of QT to Al3+ inhibits the IFE, leading to fluorescence enhancement. The N-CQDs were also effective in quantifying QT in human plasma and monitoring Al3+ in serum samples, demonstrating low toxicity toward T24 cells, making them suitable for sensing applications in living cells [253].

The arsenic oxidation As(III) is more poisonous than As(V). As (III) has a harmful effect on the environment and water quality, which can lead to health risks for humans such as respiratory and cardiovascular disorders as well as several forms of cancer [254]. Because of its greater concentration than 10 ppb in water, the WHO designated it as a worldwide environmental issue [255]. The synthesis of CQDs using quince fruit powder as a carbon precursor through a green, microwave-assisted method. The CQDs exhibit strong photoluminescence, and their emission intensity is significantly affected by the presence of certain ions, such as As³⁺ and MnO₄⁻. The sensing mechanism for arsenic involves the oxidation of As³⁺ by MnO₄⁻, which leads to changes in the FL intensity of the CQDs. This change is proportional to the concentration of As³⁺, allowing for its detection in aqueous solutions. The method shows good sensitivity, with a linear detection range for As³⁺ from 0.1 to 2 µg/mL [256]. The development of surface-passivated carbon dots (GSH-CDs) from prickly pear cactus through a green, one-pot hydrothermal process. These CDs were functionalized with glutathione, which provided abundant oxygen- and nitrogen-rich species on their surface, enabling effective chelation with As³⁺ ions. The sensing mechanism involved the FL quenching in the presence of As³⁺, making the GSH-CDs a highly sensitive and selective probe for arsenic detection. This method achieved a LOD as low as 2.3 nM, demonstrating its potential for practical applications in environmental monitoring [257].

The heavy metal cadmium is hazardous to human health and the environment since it is used so often in many industrial operations. Cadmium (II) and Cadmium (0) are the two main oxidation states in which it exists. Because of its propensity to build up in biological systems and cause harm to human health, such as kidney damage, brittle bones, and maybe cancer, the cadmium (II) ion is especially dangerous. Accurate cadmium ion detection is essential for public health and environmental monitoring. The synthesis of highly FL CDs from waste coconut coir, exhibited a QY of 48 % and high solubility in aqueous media. These CDs demonstrated a "turn-on and turn-off" fluorescence response for the detection of Cd²⁺ and Cu²⁺ ions, with LOD of 0.18 nM and 0.28 nM, respectively [258].

Similarly, cobalt also essential for the human body, excessive quantities of cobalt can have negative effects on environmental safety and health. Nitrogen-doped CQDs were synthesized from Nerium oleander L petals and the fluorescent property of the Rhodamine 6 G (Rh6G) dye and NCQDs were quenched utilizing the FRET mechanism. In this approach, NCQDs serve as the energy donor, and Rh6G acts as the energy acceptor. The FRET process is highly sensitive to the presence of Co²⁺ ions, as these ions disrupt the energy transfer between the NCQDs and Rh6G, leading to FL quenching. This quenching effect allows for the detection of Co²⁺ with a limit of detection as low as 6.45 nM [259].

The N-doped CQDs has been synthesized from quinoa saponin powder, by hydrothermal method at 200 °C for 10 h. The resulting N-doped CQDs have a small and uniform size of around 2.25 nm. The N-doped CQDs display excellent sensitivity for Co²⁺ detection, with a good linear correlation between FL intensity and Co²⁺ concentration in the range of 20–150 μM and a LOD as low as 0.49 μM [260].
